learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate comprehensive insights into the purpose and place of international events as socio-cultural, political and business phenomena.
Analyse and critically evaluate the role of international events in destination development.
Research and discuss issues experienced in planning and managing international events.
Develop and justify an international event portfolio for a city.
Collaborate in teams to explain the role of culture when staging international events.

assessments.task_description:
An in-person media framing test will be provided in Week 7.

Students will be presented in class with a previously unseen article. There will be multiple articles included in the class so that not everyone is reviewing the same article. Students will be asked to demonstrate their theoretical knowledge and critical application skills relating to media framing. Specifically, this in-class assessment will evaluate students' ability to identify, analyse, and critically evaluate media frames within event-related media content.

Students will be provided with a contemporary media article relating to an international event (approximately 600–800 words) at the beginning of the class.

Within 90 minutes students will need to:

  • Read the article carefully.
  • Answer all questions.
  • Support answers with examples and quotations from the article.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The news article and questions will be presented during class time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.assessment_title: Cross-Cultural Delegate Experience Strategy - Part A - Presentation
assessments.due_date:
Week 11 - Week 12

During Class Presentations.

Students must present without the aid of a script.
assessments.task_description:
World Association of Sport Management Conference 2028 – event overview

The World Association for Sport Management (WASM) Conference is one of the leading international gatherings for sport management scholars and practitioners. UQ and Brisbane will host the 2028 World Association for Sport Management (WASM) Conference in Brisbane from 13-16 February, 2028. The conference will bring together delegates from multiple continents, cultures, languages, and professional backgrounds. WASM conferences are specifically designed to promote global collaboration and knowledge exchange across the international sport management community. The 2028 conference organisers are expecting to attract conference delegates from Australia and New Zealand, Europe, North America, Asia, Africa, and Latin America.

Assessment task

As an International Event Experience Consultant, you have been commissioned by the conference organising committee to develop a comprehensive delegate experience strategy that enhances attendee satisfaction while demonstrating cultural intelligence. Additionally, you are tasked with leveraging the event to enhance the benefits to Brisbane.

Working in a group, your proposal - presented as a 10 minute presentation - should focus on creating memorable and inclusive experiences for international delegates before, during, and after the conference.

Part A: Cross-Cultural Delegate Experience Strategy

Develop a strategy demonstrating how the conference will accommodate delegates from diverse cultural backgrounds.

Address:

Cultural Analysis

  • Key elements of Australian culture
  • Key cultural differences likely to affect delegate expectations.
  • Potential communication barriers.
  • Cultural dimensions relevant to event participation.

Inclusive Event Design

  • Pre-event information
  • Registration and arrival experience.
  • Networking activities.
  • Conference social events.
  • Food and beverage considerations.
  • Religious and accessibility requirements.

Cultural Risk Management

Identify at least five cultural risks and propose mitigation strategies.

Examples may include:

  • Language barriers
  • Dietary misunderstandings
  • Different networking norms
  • Religious observances
  • Expectations regarding hierarchy and authority

Leveraging the event for the city of Brisbane

·      Maximise benefits to the host city

Support your discussion with relevant academic literature.

Students are to present with minimal notes. Bullet points that are briefly glanced at are acceptable. Scripts cannot be read through any form (e.g., printed, or through the aid of a digital device).

Students will have an opportunity to do a peer evaluation survey. The results of this peer evaluation may affect a student's individual results.

Please Note: The presentation will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The order and date of presentations will be determined in week 7.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Cross-Cultural Delegate Experience Strategy - Part B - Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Part B: Individual assessment 10%: Reflection and Justification after feedback – 750 – 1000 words

Provide a critical reflection on the feedback provided to all presentations in class in week 11 and 12. Based on the feedback:

Discuss:

  • Why your experiences would appeal to a global audience.
  • How the program supports international knowledge exchange.
  • How cultural intelligence contributes to event success.
  • How you have leveraged the event for the city of Brisbane
  • How feedback provided to your group would change what you proposed

Deliverables

  • Your report-style paper should be a maximum 1000 words (times new roman, 12 point font).
  • Your paper must be uploaded to Blackboard on (or before) the due date.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit the assignment through Turnitin located in the Blackboard assessment link.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction

This week we will provide a course overview and go into depth about the requirements and assessment in the course.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction This week we will provide a course overview and go into depth about the requirements and assessment in the course.

learning_activities.topic:
Culture & Subculture

Understanding culture and subculture is essential to the management of international events. This week we will use social identity theory and various theories of cultural communication and contact to understand considerations when multiple cultures come together through international events.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Culture & Subculture Understanding culture and subculture is essential to the management of international events.  This week we will use social identity theory and various theories of cultural communication and contact to understand considerations when multiple cultures come together through international events.

learning_activities.topic:
International Media

Ekka Public Holiday - Wednesday 12th August 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes.

Please watch the videos provided on international media. Please also ensure that you pay attention to international media stories and consider how they impact international events.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: International Media Ekka Public Holiday - Wednesday 12th August 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes. Please watch the videos provided on international media.  Please also ensure that you pay attention to international media stories and consider how they impact international events.

learning_activities.topic:
Media Framing workshop

Media framing is a useful theory that helps us understand how media is reported and consumed. This week we will go over the key concepts involved in media framing and will workshop the concepts through both digital and written media.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Media Framing workshop Media framing is a useful theory that helps us understand how media is reported and consumed.  This week we will go over the key concepts involved in media framing and will workshop the concepts through both digital and written media.

learning_activities.topic:
Event leveraging and non-host leveraging

Events do not automatically create impact in host destinations. Event leveraging theory helps us understand how to strategically maximise benefits from events. This week we will use Chalip's (2004) leveraging model on event leveraging to discuss how events can be leveraged for tourism, economic, and social benefits.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Event leveraging and non-host leveraging Events do not automatically create impact in host destinations. Event leveraging theory helps us understand how to strategically maximise benefits from events. This week we will use Chalip's (2004) leveraging model on event leveraging to discuss how events can be leveraged for tourism, economic, and social benefits.

learning_activities.topic:
Leveraging events to enhance accessibility and inclusion

Accessibility and inclusion are two key areas that international event managers should understand. This week we will use the principle of user centred design to understand how events can be leveraged to enhance accessibility and inclusion.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Leveraging events to enhance accessibility and inclusion
learning_activities.topic_description: Leveraging events to enhance accessibility and inclusion Accessibility and inclusion are two key areas that international event managers should understand.  This week we will use the principle of user centred design to understand how events can be leveraged to enhance accessibility and inclusion.

learning_activities.topic:
Media Framing assessment in class

Media Framing assessment will be conducted in this class session in Week 7. You will be presented with a number of media articles to analyse. See assessment link for further description.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Media Framing assessment in class Media Framing assessment will be conducted in this class session in Week 7. You will be presented with a number of media articles to analyse. See assessment link for further description.

learning_activities.topic:
Bidding for international events

For many international events, cities must first go through a competitive process of bidding for the event. This week we will discuss the process of assessing whether a city should bid for an event, as well as the process of putting together a competitive bid.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Bidding for international events For many international events, cities must first go through a competitive process of bidding for the event.  This week we will discuss the process of assessing whether a city should bid for an event, as well as the process of putting together a competitive bid.

learning_activities.topic:
Volunteers at international events

Volunteers are often the cornerstone of international events. Volunteering is not a culturally universal - it has different meanings and process across countries and cultures. This week we will discuss the cultural meaning of volunteering and discuss how to recruit and retain volunteers in different countries.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Volunteers at international events Volunteers are often the cornerstone of international events. Volunteering is not a culturally universal - it has different meanings and process across countries and cultures. This week we will discuss the cultural meaning of volunteering and discuss how to recruit and retain volunteers in different countries.

learning_activities.topic:
Politics and human rights

Understanding politics that influence the bidding for and hosting international events is important. Additionally, human rights are often a core concern raised around the hosting of international events. This week we will discuss issues around politics and human rights.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Politics and human rights Understanding politics that influence the bidding for and hosting international events is important. Additionally, human rights are often a core concern raised around the hosting of international events.  This week we will discuss issues around politics and human rights.

learning_activities.topic:
Soft power

International events are often used as, and for, a form of cultural diplomacy. This week we will discuss how soft power can be used at and through events.

Group presentations will also take place this week.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Soft power International events are often used as, and for, a form of cultural diplomacy.  This week we will discuss how soft power can be used at and through events. Group presentations will also take place this week.

Group presentations
